title: Fig. 8. Comparison of measured and model-calculated source strengths of benzene for 12 BAB II periods.
references: During the BAB II campaign, over 50 NMHCs were detected (Petrea et al., 2005). The source strengths of 21 NMHC compounds could be calculated and were compared with model calculations by Kuhlwein and Friedrich (2005). For benzene the comparison of measured and model-calculated source strengths are given in Fig. 8 for 12 time intervals between 2 and  $4\mathrm{h}$  duration. There are cases of over- and underestimated model-calculated source strength compared to the measurements. On average the measurements are  $26\%$  higher than the model calculations for highest stage 3.

The quality of an emission calculation model based on emission factors measured on roller test stands and statistical traffic data was evaluated using source strengths and emission factors calculated from real-world exhaust gas concentration differences measured upwind and downwind of a motorway in southwest Germany. Gaseous and particulate emissions were taken into account. Detailed traffic census data were taken during the measurements. The results were compared with findings of similar studies.

The source strengths of 21 non-methane hydrocarbon (NMHC) compounds quantified are underestimated by the model. The ratio between the measured and model-calculated emissions ranges from 1.3 to 2.1 for BTX and up to 21 for 16 other NMHCs. The reason for the differences is the insufficient knowledge of NMHC emissions of road traffic.

The scope of this final paper within the special issue on the results of the BAB II project is (i) to summarise the main results, (ii) to compare the measured and model-calculated source strengths of emissions, released by traffic on the motorway section under investigation and finally to evaluate the quality of the emission calculation model updated by the results of the traffic census made simultaneously. Although emission factors for a "mean vehicle" depending on the fleet composition passing the measurement site are given, the main objective is the improvement of the emission model by reliable traffic data and not by updated emission factors. These can not be estimated by the method used in BAB II, because the model needs emission factors of individual vehicle types (motors) as input data rather than emission factors valid for a fleet of vehicles passing the site randomly distributed. The remaining differences between measured and model-calculated source strengths is therefore mainly attributed to imprecise or even missing emission factors for gases, NMHC compounds and particulates from roller test stands.

The efficiency of a controlled 3-way catalytic converter in a passenger car and cold-start emissions with respect to benzene, toluene and xylene (BTX) emissions in particular was investigated by Heeb et al. (2000, 2003). Free air measurements were made by Leisen et al. (1992) on a motorway in Germany and by Kittelson et al. (2004) in the US. In the former BAB 656 study by Vogel et al. (2000), model-calculated emissions using real-world concentration differences between the lee and the windward side of the motorway were evaluated. The deficiencies of BAB 656 consisted in the estimation of the CO and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  plume height using  $\mathrm{O}_3$  as indicator and in comparing measured and calculated emissions for three 1-h intervals only. As there are indications of traffic-induced turbulence being important to the dispersion of emissions close to the source (Eskridge and Rao, 1983; Rao et al., 2002), additionally horizontal and vertical profiles of turbulence were measured in BAB II.

As this paper summarises the BAB II results and compares measured and model calculated emissions, for detailed information about the determination of source strengths, traffic census data and model development look up the specific contributions within this special issue. For the overall project description see Corsmeier et al. (2005). The source strengths for CO and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  are given by Kohler et al. (2005), those for NMHC compounds by Petrea et al. (2005). PM emissions are investigated by Rosenbohm et al. (2005) and Imhof et al. (2005) and finally the emission model calculations and the traffic measurements are presented by Kuhlwein and Friedrich (2005).

The measured emissions and the emissions calculated by the model are compared in this paper. Irrespective of the results of this comparison, however, the BAB II study is much more elaborate in comparison to tunnel studies. The circumstances under which the emissions are calculated are more realistic than in a tunnel. On the

Fig. 2. Measured and model-calculated weekly cycle of mean source strengths of CO and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  between the morning and evening rush hours, averaged over the time period of the BAB II campaign. The error bars indicate the standard error.

largest mixing ratios, whereas iso-pentane and propene reach the largest mixing ratios of alkanes and alkenes, respectively. Typical downwind and upwind concentration differences amount to  $1.00\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  toluene and  $0.35\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  benzene, depending on the traffic load. Vertical gradients are observed for several NMHCs downwind of the motorway, which are of the same magnitude as the downwind-upwind gradients near the surface. On the windward side, the NMHC profiles are vertically constant. This indicates the existence of a plume of NMHCs emitted by road traffic. Discrete vertical NMHC measurements at up to five different heights were correlated with the continuous vertical profiles for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , in order to generate continuous vertical profiles for the individual NMHCs (Petrea et al., 2005). From the continuous vertical profiles of NMHCs and meteorological data, the emission source strength is calculated for a variety of NMHCs for time periods on working days and on a weekend. In most cases, the source strength follows the traffic density. For toluene, a maximum value of  $138.9\pm 41.7\mathrm{gkm}^{-1}\mathrm{h}^{-1}$  is calculated for a working day morning rush hour, while the minimum is  $38.0\pm 11.4\mathrm{gkm}^{-1}\mathrm{h}^{-1}$  on a Saturday afternoon. For benzene, the range of calculated source strengths is from  $47.7\pm 14.3$  to  $17.6\pm 5.3\mathrm{gkm}^{-1}\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ , whereas i-pentane has a maximum source strength

of  $241.7 \pm 72.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~km}^{-1} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$  and a minimum of  $53.8 \pm 16.4 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~km}^{-1} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$ . The measured and model calculated source strengths of benzene and toluene on Wednesday, 8 May 2001, are shown in Fig. 3 for four time periods of varying traffic load on the motorway. An almost constant toluene/benzene mass ratio of 2.2 is obtained from measurements and 1.9 from model calculations, as was expected from literature.
Fig. 3. Daily cycles of measured and model calculated source strengths of benzene, toluene, and the toluene/benzene ratio for a working day (8 May 2001).

Vehicle emissions calculated from exhaust gas concentration measurements in ambient air along the motorway A656 are compared to emissions determined by an emission calculation model using emission factors (UBA, 1999), statistical data on car fleet and traffic flow from common databases, and detailed and sophisticated traffic census data as described by Kuhlwein and Friedrich (2005). When using all BAB II quality-checked data, the measured emissions exceed the model-calculated CO and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  source intensities on all days (Figs. 2 and 7). The CO emissions are underestimated by the model by  $33\%$  on weekends and by  $20\%$  on working

Fig. 7. Derivation of measured and model calculated source strengths of CO and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for all days, working days, and weekends in percent. No differentiation of LDV and HDV was made in this case. The model underestimates the source strengths between  $17\%$  and  $33\%$ .

(John et al., 1999; Hausberger et al., 2003). The consequence is that the general underestimation of  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  source strengths in real-world traffic is mainly caused by an underestimation of heavy-duty emissions. On motorways with a higher HDV load, this underestimation will be even more important than on motorway A 656, where the difference between LDV and all vehicles emission factors is  $0.40\mathrm{gkm}^{-1}\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  only, if measured and  $0.33\mathrm{gkm}^{-1}\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ , if model-calculated.

Excluding the data of 8 May, 20-22 Central European Summer Time (CEST), a period with poorly fulfilled prerequisites for source strength calculations, the difference between measurements and calculations is reduced to  $18\%$ . This finding underlines the relatively good emission factors for benzene from roller test stand investigations compared to most of the other NMHC compounds. The comparison of all 21 ratios of measured and model-calculated source strengths is shown in Fig. 9. In general, the measured values are higher than the modelled ones. Overall 17 measured NMHC compounds are in a range of 1.3-7 times higher than modelled, whereas 4 compounds are between 11 and 21 times higher than the modelled ones. The measured value for i-butane, benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene and 1,2,4-trimethylbenzene/decane are within the standard deviation in a good agreement with the modelled values. For the other compounds the difference between model results and measurements is higher than the standard deviation.
Fig. 9. Comparison of the ratios of measured and model-calculated source strengths of 21 NMHC species measured during BAB II.

The calculations of source strengths and the evaluation of emission factors were carried out for major components of traffic emissions, i.e. CO,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and 21 NMHC compounds. An important outcome of BAB II is that, in general, the measured emissions of both gaseous compounds CO and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  are larger than the model-calculated emissions by  $23\%$  and  $17\%$ , respectively. Using multiple regression, it can be shown that the major discrepancy between  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  simulations and measurements is caused by HDV. This indicates that the emission factors given for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  are still too low, especially for HDV  $(22\%)$ . For CO, the HDV and LDV fractions could not be separated, but it may be assumed that the CO emission factor is influenced mainly by LDV and that the given emission factor also is too low  $(23\%)$ .
concise_summary: The figure shows a comparison of measured and model-calculated benzene source strengths over 12 periods during the BAB II campaign.
inferred_insight: The figure highlights discrepancies between measured and model-calculated benzene emissions, often showing underestimation by the model compared to actual measurements.

During BAB II, more than 50 NMHCs were detected and the source strengths of 21 of them were quantified (Petrea et al., 2005). Horizontal distribution of C4-C9 aliphatic and aromatic hydrocarbons on both sides of the motorway was monitored using a quasi on-line gas chromatograph (GC). Vertical NMHC concentration profiles were determined in 3 heights upwind and in 5 heights downwind of the motorway by means of adsorption tubes that were analysed by off-line GC. Good agreement between the quasi on-line and the offline measurements was found for 10 compounds measured with both systems. Generally, the relative uncertainty for these NMHCs ranged between  $10\%$  and  $30\%$  depending on the concentration level (Vogt et al., 2005).

title: Figure 1. Methodology framework for fusing multisource data to derive daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plants in China.
references: Figure 1 shows the methodology framework in this study, including the input database and processes to fuse multisource data to derive daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plant emissions.

In this work, we develop a framework that fuses plant-level daily profiles from CEMS with yearly emission estimates from CPED to obtain daily emissions (Figure 1). According to guideline HJ/T 75-2017, the maximum volume fraction of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions from the combustion of specific fuels remains stable, so we use daily profiles of flue gas volume that takes the oxygen content into account to describe daily variations in  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions. Daily profiles of emission rates from CEMS are used for major air pollutants of  $\mathrm{SO}_{2}, \mathrm{NO}_{x},$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ .
# Daily Emission Patterns of Coal-Fired Power Plants in China Based on Multisource Data Fusion

ABSTRACT: Daily emission estimates are essential for tracking the dynamic changes in emission sources. In this work, we estimate daily emissions of coal-fired power plants in China during 2017-2020 by combining information from the unit-based China coal-fired Power plant Emissions Database (CPED) and real-time measurements from continuous emission monitoring systems (CEMS). We develop a step-by-step method to screen outliers and impute missing values for data from CEMS. Then, plant-level daily profiles of flue gas volume and emissions obtained from CEMS are coupled with annual emissions from CPED to derive daily emissions. Reasonable agreement is found between emission variations and available statistics (i.e., monthly power generation and daily coal consumption). Daily power emissions are in the range of 6267-12,994, 0.4-1.3, 6.5-12.0, and  $2.5 - 6.8\mathrm{Gg}$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_{x}$ , and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , respectively, with high emissions in winter and summer caused

is used to quantify monthly variations in power emissions in a unit-level power plant emission inventory in China.17 Indicator data such as power load curves are employed to indicate daily or hourly dynamics of power emissions.1,18 Recent studies have also attempted to estimate dynamically updated daily power emissions,19-22 which are largely stimulated by the demand for quantifying the evolution of emissions during fast-evolving events (e.g., COVID-19 pandemic). Liu et al.19,20 used daily coal consumption from six major power generation groups in China to estimate daily updated power emissions. However, the daily coal consumption data only covers 62 power plants in China, and the activity indicator-based method is relatively indirect and lacks independent evaluations against observations. Studies utilizing satellite observations to estimate daily emission changes are faced with the challenges of differentiating power emissions from the total21,23 or deriving daily power emissions on continuous time scales.24,25 For example, Ding et al.23 used the  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission reductions derived from

In this work, taking advantage of a well-validated unit-based power emission database covering China and the high temporal resolution of CEMS data, we attempt to construct daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plants in China based on multisource data fusion. Careful screening of outliers and imputation of missing values are conducted for the flue gas volume and emission data. Then, the plant-level daily profiles (i.e., coefficients for day-to-day variations in a year) from CEMS are combined with the unit-level annual emissions from

2.1.1. Unit-Level Emission Database of Coal-Fired Power Plants in China. The CPED, $^{17}$  developed by Tsinghua University, is a high-resolution emission inventory that provides year-by-year emissions from coal-fired power plants since 1990 in China. Established based on detailed and unit-level information from the Ministry of Ecology and Environment (MEE, undisclosed data), CPED contains unit locations, operational status, unit capacity, coal consumption, control technologies, and emission factors, which enable accurate spatial allocation and emission estimates for power plants. More details about CPED can be found in the paper by Liu et al. $^{16}$  Previous studies have verified that the magnitude and trends of power emissions in CPED are in good agreement with top-down estimates from satellite measurements. $^{43,50}$  Liu et al. $^{43}$  compared  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions in CPED with those based on satellite data and found rather good agreement with the relative differences of  $4 \pm 18\%$ . CPED used here covers 30 provinces (except Hong Kong, Macao, Taiwan, and Tibet), 3948 power plants, and 8722 in-use electricity-generating units from 2017 to 2020.

In this study, data from CPED and CEMS are fused to improve the daily estimation of emissions from coal-fired power units in China. The comprehensive, highly resolved emission database could capture the dynamic changes in emissions during typical socioeconomic events and could be applied to providing more accurate inputs for CTMs and guiding future policies.

In previous studies, monthly activity data (e.g., electricity generation and fuel consumption) $^{4}$  and invariable parameters considering factors such as working times $^{1,57}$  have been widely used to disaggregate annual power emissions into monthly and daily scales, respectively. Figure 5 shows comparisons of the multiscale temporal profiles from CEMS with the widely used temporal profiles. At the monthly scale (Figure 5a), the comparison between national average profiles obtained from CEMS and electricity generation shows good agreement ( $R$  between 0.74 and 0.97). The results imply that the commonly used activity indicators are reliable at monthly scale when direct emission measurements are unavailable. At the weekly scale, previous studies $^{1,57}$  use fixed parameters that do not vary over time to allocate emissions from weekly totals to daily scales, considering the empirical production schedule and a few observations, which tend to fail to reflect fluctuations caused by unexpected events. Distinct weekly patterns of power emissions in previous studies $^{1,57}$  are presented in Figure 5b, which are manifested as strong intensity of production activities on weekdays and weak intensity on weekends. Conversely, the weekly profiles from CEMS exhibit no obvious weekend effects, showing that the widely used profiles might exaggerate the differences between weekdays and weekends. Indeed, in many developed countries, $^{58}$  power demand on working days is usually higher than that on weekends. However, owing to different lifestyle, economic, and cultural background, there is no significant difference in electricity demand and power generation between weekdays and weekends in China. And unlike peaking power plants (e.g., natural gas power plants and hydroelectric facilities), coal-fired

In this work, we estimate daily emissions from coal-fired power plants in China during 2017-2020 through data fusion of a well-validated unit-based power emission database and high-temporal-resolution measurements from CEMS. A step-by-step method is developed to screen outliers and impute missing values for data from CEMS, and sensitivity tests show that monthly and weekly temporal profiles by different imputation methods are in good agreement ( $R = 0.90 - 0.98$  for national scale,  $R = 0.61 - 0.92$  for provincial level). We find that the temporal profiles from CEMS agree well with available statistical data like monthly power generation and daily coal consumption, with  $R$  greater than 0.7 for 16-27 out of 30 provinces at the monthly scale and  $R$  up to 0.83 at the daily scale. Daily power emissions from the fused database are in the range of 6267-12,994, 0.4-1.3, 6.5-12.0, and  $2.5 - 6.8\mathrm{Gg}$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , respectively, during 2017-2020, reflecting higher emissions in winter and summer caused by heating and cooling demand and lower ones resulting from holidays. Our estimates are also able to capture the sudden changes in daily power emissions during typical socioeconomic events, including the much slower rebound of power emissions after the Spring Festival holiday due to the impact of COVID-19, higher daily  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions from thermal power generation in Yunnan owing to a severe drought, and significant decline of power emissions in Wuhan caused by a short-term emission control during the 7th CISM Military World Games. We also find that previous studies (e.g.,  $\mathrm{EDGAR}^1$ ) that apply fixed parameters to allocate weekly power emission totals to daily scales exaggerate the differences between weekdays and weekends. This work provides a framework to propagate information from real-time emission measurements to high-temporal-resolution emission estimates, which will support dynamic emission tracking and air pollution modeling.

title: Figure 5. Comparisons of monthly and weekly profiles based on flue gas volume or emission rates from CEMS and those widely used in previous studies. (a) Monthly temporal profiles derived from national  SO_2  emission,  NO_x  emission,  PM_{2.5}  emission, and flue gas volume data from CEMS or electricity generation. The electricity-generation data is accessed from the National Bureau of Statistics (https://data.stats.gov.cn/). (b) Weekly temporal profiles calculated by national average flue gas volume,  SO_2  emission,  NO_x  emission, and  PM_{2.5}  emission data from CEMS or from previous studies including that of He et al. ^{57}  and the EDGAR. ^1
references: In previous studies, monthly activity data (e.g., electricity generation and fuel consumption) $^{4}$  and invariable parameters considering factors such as working times $^{1,57}$  have been widely used to disaggregate annual power emissions into monthly and daily scales, respectively. Figure 5 shows comparisons of the multiscale temporal profiles from CEMS with the widely used temporal profiles. At the monthly scale (Figure 5a), the comparison between national average profiles obtained from CEMS and electricity generation shows good agreement ( $R$  between 0.74 and 0.97). The results imply that the commonly used activity indicators are reliable at monthly scale when direct emission measurements are unavailable. At the weekly scale, previous studies $^{1,57}$  use fixed parameters that do not vary over time to allocate emissions from weekly totals to daily scales, considering the empirical production schedule and a few observations, which tend to fail to reflect fluctuations caused by unexpected events. Distinct weekly patterns of power emissions in previous studies $^{1,57}$  are presented in Figure 5b, which are manifested as strong intensity of production activities on weekdays and weak intensity on weekends. Conversely, the weekly profiles from CEMS exhibit no obvious weekend effects, showing that the widely used profiles might exaggerate the differences between weekdays and weekends. Indeed, in many developed countries, $^{58}$  power demand on working days is usually higher than that on weekends. However, owing to different lifestyle, economic, and cultural background, there is no significant difference in electricity demand and power generation between weekdays and weekends in China. And unlike peaking power plants (e.g., natural gas power plants and hydroelectric facilities), coal-fired

Figure 5. Comparisons of monthly and weekly profiles based on flue gas volume or emission rates from CEMS and those widely used in previous studies. (a) Monthly temporal profiles derived from national  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emission,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission, and flue gas volume data from CEMS or electricity generation. The electricity-generation data is accessed from the National Bureau of Statistics (https://data.stats.gov.cn/). (b) Weekly temporal profiles calculated by national average flue gas volume,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emission,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission, and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission data from CEMS or from previous studies including that of He et al. $^{57}$  and the EDGAR. $^1$
concise_summary: Figure 5 shows monthly and weekly profiles of emissions and flue gas volume from CEMS compared with previous studies, illustrating differences in temporal patterns.
inferred_insight: CEMS profiles align well with electricity generation data for monthly scales but reveal discrepancies in weekly patterns, challenging previous studies' assumptions about weekday and weekend emission differences.

title: Figure 1. Contributions of various types of compounds to the total U.S. emissions mixture. The numbers of compounds of the various types are given in parentheses.
references: The model species assignment database currently includes over 1850 chemical categories that refer either to individual organic compounds or (in about 70 cases) to mixtures of higher-molecular-weight (primarily  $C_{\geq 12}$ ) isomers that are represented in the same way in all the profile categories and the currently supported mechanisms. For simplicity, all of these are referred to as compounds in the subsequent discussion, to distinguish them from known or unknown mixture categories that are discussed separately below. The numbers of compounds with various chemical structures are shown on Figure 1, which also gives their mole fractions in a total U.S. emissions mixture, discussed below. Approximately half the compounds are hydrocarbons, approximately  $1/3$  are various oxygenates, and approximately  $8\%$  of the compounds contain nitrogen. Note that there are large numbers of types of compounds besides just alkanes, alkenes, aromatic hydrocarbons, and simple oxygenates.

precursor (U.S. Code of Federal Regulations, 2014), flags indicating whether the compound is judged to be volatile and whether it is assigned model species, and the model species assignments for the various mechanisms. To aid in making model species assignments, structural information is also given for many of the compounds, using the designations shown on Figure 1.

The mass fractions of various types of compounds in total U.S. emissions mixture are shown in Figure 1. The mass fractions are generally similar for the other anthropogenic mixtures, as shown in Table S-11 in the SM. The alkanes are the most important for the anthropogenic mixtures, representing  $37 - 46\%$  of the mass. Alkenes, aromatics, alcohols, and other oxygenates are next in importance but have different

The development of a database for making model species assignments when preparing total organic gas (TOG) emissions input for atmospheric models is described. This database currently has assignments of model species for 12 different gas-phase chemical mechanisms for over 1700 chemical compounds and covers over 3000 chemical categories used in five different anthropogenic TOG profile databases or output by two different biogenic emissions models. This involved developing a unified chemical classification system, assigning compounds to mixtures, assigning model species for the mechanisms to the compounds, and making assignments for unknown, unassigned, and nonvolatile mass. The comprehensiveness of the assignments, the contributions of various types of speciation categories to current profile and total emissions data, inconsistencies with existing undocumented model species assignments, and remaining speciation issues and areas of needed work are also discussed. The use of the system to prepare input for SMOKE, the Speciation Tool, and for biogenic models is described in the supplementary materials. The database, associated programs and files, and a users manual are available online at http://www.cert.ucr.edu/~carter/emitdb.

The first step in developing this model species assignment database was to compile a master list of all the chemical categories used in the all the supported databases to specify the chemical compositions of the various profiles. This master list is given in a table in the database along with separate tables giving mappings of chemical category codes used in the various speciation profile databases to the categories in the master list. Although most of the categories in the unified list refer to single organic chemical compounds, a significant number refers to mixtures of varying degrees of complexity and specificity, including many where the compositions could not be determined. The types of categories used and the numbers of categories of each type are listed in Table 3. The approach used in

The list of profile and total emissions mixtures used to assess the speciation category assignments in this work is given in Table 4. These consist of profile totals (e.g., "S44 Prof") where each profile is weighted equally, anthropogenic emissions totals (e.g., "US Emit") where the profiles are weighted by the total mass of TOG emissions in various domains assigned to the profiles, and biogenic emissions totals predicted by the BEIS or MEGAN models. The emissions totals are relevant to assessing relative importance in model inputs, whereas the profile totals are useful when assessing contributions of one type of source relative to others, including sources with currently low emissions that may become relatively more important in the future.

Table 5 summarizes the mass fractions of the various types of chemical categories in the profile and emissions total mixtures. It can be seen that the assignments for the lumped mechanisms are quite comprehensive, with over  $95\%$  of the mass of anthropogenic emissions, over  $92\%$  of the mass of the anthropogenic profiles, and all of the mass of the biogenic mixtures assigned model species in the lumped mechanisms. Most of the emissions and profiles are assigned to single-compound categories, but the contribution of mixture categories is non-negligible, ranging from  $9\%$  to  $15\%$  of the mass for the anthropogenic mixtures. The mass contributions of compounds assumed to be nonvolatile (which are counted as assigned for the purpose of modeling) are nonzero but relatively small, being  $0.4\%$  or less for the emissions or profile totals. The contributions of unspecified volatile mixture categories are relatively small, ranging from  $\sim 2\%$  for California emissions totals to  $\sim 8\%$  for the Asia emissions and SPECIATE 4.4 profiles. The contributions of unassigned compounds, poorly defined categories, and unclassified categories are negligible in all these cases.

rankings of relative importance in the four anthropogenic emissions mixtures. The relative importance of the other types of compounds is even more variable, especially those with relatively low contributions to the total mass. Alkenes (primarily isoprene and terpenes) are the most important for the biogenic mixtures, but other types of compounds are non-negligible, as shown on Table S-11 in the SM.

Figure 2 shows that the molar emissions of CB05 PAR, the model species used to represent "paraffinic" carbons, are much higher than any other model species emissions in either mechanism. This is because PAR represents carbons in many types of molecules. Figure 3 shows the distribution of types of compounds with carbons represented by PAR in the total U.S. emissions mixture. A significant fraction ( $\sim 27\%$ ) of the carbon represented by PAR represents carbons in compounds other than alkanes. Note that the mechanisms for PAR were originally derived based primarily on mechanisms for alkanes that are represented by ALK3 and ALK4 (Gery et al., 1988).

title: Figure 2. Moles of SAPRC-07 and CB05 model species per kilogram for total U.S, California, Texas, or Asia anthropogenic TOG emissions.
references: The model species assignment database currently includes over 1850 chemical categories that refer either to individual organic compounds or (in about 70 cases) to mixtures of higher-molecular-weight (primarily  $C_{\geq 12}$ ) isomers that are represented in the same way in all the profile categories and the currently supported mechanisms. For simplicity, all of these are referred to as compounds in the subsequent discussion, to distinguish them from known or unknown mixture categories that are discussed separately below. The numbers of compounds with various chemical structures are shown on Figure 1, which also gives their mole fractions in a total U.S. emissions mixture, discussed below. Approximately half the compounds are hydrocarbons, approximately  $1/3$  are various oxygenates, and approximately  $8\%$  of the compounds contain nitrogen. Note that there are large numbers of types of compounds besides just alkanes, alkenes, aromatic hydrocarbons, and simple oxygenates.
Figure 1. Contributions of various types of compounds to the total U.S. emissions mixture. The numbers of compounds of the various types are given in parentheses.

precursor (U.S. Code of Federal Regulations, 2014), flags indicating whether the compound is judged to be volatile and whether it is assigned model species, and the model species assignments for the various mechanisms. To aid in making model species assignments, structural information is also given for many of the compounds, using the designations shown on Figure 1.

The mass fractions of various types of compounds in total U.S. emissions mixture are shown in Figure 1. The mass fractions are generally similar for the other anthropogenic mixtures, as shown in Table S-11 in the SM. The alkanes are the most important for the anthropogenic mixtures, representing  $37 - 46\%$  of the mass. Alkenes, aromatics, alcohols, and other oxygenates are next in importance but have different

Table 5 summarizes the mass fractions of the various types of chemical categories in the profile and emissions total mixtures. It can be seen that the assignments for the lumped mechanisms are quite comprehensive, with over  $95\%$  of the mass of anthropogenic emissions, over  $92\%$  of the mass of the anthropogenic profiles, and all of the mass of the biogenic mixtures assigned model species in the lumped mechanisms. Most of the emissions and profiles are assigned to single-compound categories, but the contribution of mixture categories is non-negligible, ranging from  $9\%$  to  $15\%$  of the mass for the anthropogenic mixtures. The mass contributions of compounds assumed to be nonvolatile (which are counted as assigned for the purpose of modeling) are nonzero but relatively small, being  $0.4\%$  or less for the emissions or profile totals. The contributions of unspecified volatile mixture categories are relatively small, ranging from  $\sim 2\%$  for California emissions totals to  $\sim 8\%$  for the Asia emissions and SPECIATE 4.4 profiles. The contributions of unassigned compounds, poorly defined categories, and unclassified categories are negligible in all these cases.

title: Figure 3. Mole fractions of carbons in various types of compounds in the U.S. total emissions mixture that are represented by the CB05 PAR model species. Alkanes are classified their representation in the SAPRC-07 condensed mechanism.
references: Figure 2 shows that the molar emissions of CB05 PAR, the model species used to represent "paraffinic" carbons, are much higher than any other model species emissions in either mechanism. This is because PAR represents carbons in many types of molecules. Figure 3 shows the distribution of types of compounds with carbons represented by PAR in the total U.S. emissions mixture. A significant fraction ( $\sim 27\%$ ) of the carbon represented by PAR represents carbons in compounds other than alkanes. Note that the mechanisms for PAR were originally derived based primarily on mechanisms for alkanes that are represented by ALK3 and ALK4 (Gery et al., 1988).

The model species assignment database currently includes over 1850 chemical categories that refer either to individual organic compounds or (in about 70 cases) to mixtures of higher-molecular-weight (primarily  $C_{\geq 12}$ ) isomers that are represented in the same way in all the profile categories and the currently supported mechanisms. For simplicity, all of these are referred to as compounds in the subsequent discussion, to distinguish them from known or unknown mixture categories that are discussed separately below. The numbers of compounds with various chemical structures are shown on Figure 1, which also gives their mole fractions in a total U.S. emissions mixture, discussed below. Approximately half the compounds are hydrocarbons, approximately  $1/3$  are various oxygenates, and approximately  $8\%$  of the compounds contain nitrogen. Note that there are large numbers of types of compounds besides just alkanes, alkenes, aromatic hydrocarbons, and simple oxygenates.

title: Fig. 2. Heating area and coal consumption (1997-2018).
references: Due to the lower temperatures in Northern China, burning coal for heating had become the most common way in which residents kept warm in winter. Since the 1950s, the Government of China provided winter heating via coal-fired boilers in the north Huai River areas. Northern cities received free unlimited heating from November 15 to March 15. Up until now, heating has not only been an important indicator of social wealth but has also become necessary for the survival of northern people in winter. According to statistical data released by the Ministry of Housing and Urban-Rural Development (MOHURD) of the People's Republic of China, the annual growth rate of the urban centralized heating area reached  $14.04\%$  during 1991-2017 (Fig. 2). At the same time, coal consumption increased under the rising demand for heating in the two decades prior to 2013. The "coal-to-gas/electricity" policy has generally altered the previous energy consumption structure. However, the rural heating energy consumption structure has largely remained unbalanced, among which coal consumption accounts for approximately  $75\%$ . Moreover, the large scattered coal heating demand has caused a series of environmental and social problems. The combustion of coal in boilers is associated with the release of air pollutants, especially  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and total suspended particles (TSP). Winter heating has also reduced people's life expectancy in northern regions of China [27]. To meet the increasing demand for heating as well as to alleviate air pollutant emissions, the National Development and Reform Commission of the People's Republic of China announced its plan for the WCHP project (covering 2017-2021).

implementing intervention measures have seen substantial air quality improvements [7,8]. For example, the United States of America has replaced coal with natural gas in generating electric power since 1997 and has achieved  $44\%$  and  $40\%$  reduction in  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emissions from the 1997 levels [9]. A cleaner heating technologies program in New Zealand has led to the significant decline of PM emissions and concentrations [10]. Such policies aiming to diversify energy structures with clean and renewable energy have been successful in certain countries, which also has important implications for China [11]. In Northern China, the annual consumption of coal used for heating is approximately 400 million metric tons, nearly  $50\%$  of which is separately distributed for use in rural areas. Moreover, the combustion of scattered coal heating has caused severe haze to occur in winter [12]. Chen et al. found that the combustion of scattered coal heating has led to an approximately two-thirds increase in air pollutants during periods when heating is employed (i.e., winter) compared to periods when heating is not employed (i.e., summer) [13]. Compared to coal, clean energies such as natural gas and wind power emit less  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , and  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ , and thus reduce human health risks from exposure to air pollutants [9]. Therefore, switching from scattered coal

Studies on the effectiveness of energy transition have not produced a consensus among scholars. Lin and Zhu [18] found that fuel switching in "energy saving and emission reduction" (ESER) policy had failed to decrease  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations. Li and Chen [19] reported that a city that underwent coal-to-gas transition had no significant impact on reducing  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions, and this was primarily because the city focused on the replacement of industrial coal while ignoring the combustion of scattered coal heating in rural areas. Additionally, although Zhao and Luo [20] reported that a "coal-to-gas" project decreased pollutant emissions from a household sector by substituting civil urban coal consumption, they confirmed that this project had a limited effect on reducing industrial coal consumption. Liu et al. [21] adopted the GAINS model to examine the effect of coal switching strategy in Jing-Jin-Ji region, and estimated that the primary  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  decreased by  $28\%$  and  $11\%$ , respectively. Wu et al. [22] evaluated the household coal-replacement program in North China and concluded that coal-to-electricity policy achieved the goal of a clean winter, while the high-quality coal replacement policy had no improvement in indoor air quality. Since the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (smog) crisis in the winter of 2013, environmental governance in China has shifted attention to scattered coal heating in rural areas, after which a series of policies were implemented and subsequently achieved remarkable results [23]. However, official reports may have overestimated the benefits of the clean heating policy by simply attributing the decrease in air pollutant concentrations to the promotion of clean heating policies while ignoring the interference of other exogenous energy-saving and emission-reduction policies [24]. Additionally, policy impacts will vary in different regions; thus, it is necessary to identify the validity of such policy among regions. Few studies to date have focused on the evaluation of energy transition policies in China. As an important part of China's energy transition process, it must be determined that whether or not the "Winter Clean Heating Pilot" (WCHP) project improves air quality. Answering this question can offer some guidance in China's future environmental governance while also providing a quantitative reference for other coal-based, energy-consuming developing countries. It could also be beneficial to achieve the synergy between emission reduction and air quality improvement under the background of climate change.

On the one hand, even though the WCHP project has proven to be an effective mean to control winter-derived heating emissions, the cost of this project should not be ignored. The conversion cost of "coal-to-gas/electricity" is higher and includes a subsequent increase in the cost of gas. Replacing unprocessed coal with clean coal can be used as a transitional method in the ultimate goal of energy transition for regions that experience considerable financial governmental pressure and low residential income. The government should focus on reforming the cost of electricity/gas by updating to more advanced heating technology and devices. On the other hand, since the reduction effect of the WCHP project on regional air quality varies under different development levels and resource distributions, project control should be delegated to regional governments based on local features rather than following the outdated pattern of "one-size-fits-all". In order to minimize the negative impact of clean heating policies on energy poverty and energy consumption inequality, the government should provide subsidies to clean energy more affordable to low-income groups.

title: Fig. 3. reduction effects of the WCHP project on major pollutants in eastern and western region.
references: In order to intuitively illustrate the effects of the WCHP project on air pollutants, we separately calculated percentage reduction effects of this policy in the eastern and western regions (Fig. 3). In the eastern region, decreasing percentages of AQI,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , and  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  were  $15.51\%$ ,  $14.72\%$ ,  $71.08\%$ , and  $31.22\%$ , respectively. In the western region, corresponding decreasing percentages were  $25.02\%$ ,  $22.25\%$ ,  $65.43\%$ , and  $36.49\%$ , respectively. Decreases of most

Coal used for heating rural households poses a severe threat to ambient air pollution and human health. In 2017, China implemented the Winter Clean Heating Pilot (WCHP) project, which was designed to convert energy from "coal-to-gas/electricity". However, the impact of the WCHP project and its associated dynamic changes have not yet been well quantified. Based on daily monitored panel data covering 35 pilot cities, we conducted a difference-in-differences (DID) model to identify whether or not the WCHP project is effective in maintaining air quality. Results showed that AQI,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ , and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions decreased by  $20.4\%$ ,  $18.59\%$ ,  $34.1\%$ , and  $68.4\%$ , respectively, and, except for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , these pollutant reductions were greater in Western China than in Eastern China. Furthermore, when certain variables were controlled, such as weather, holidays, GDP, the ratio between secondary industries, income levels, and environmental expenditures, these results were also robust. Parallel trends and placebo tests were additionally used to confirm the reliability of the DID model. The implementation of energy transition could gain the synergy between emission reduction and air quality improvement. Policy recommendations were finally made on rural-scale energy transition, including reforming the cost of electricity/gas through advanced technology and subsidies to account for regional inequality.

Table 4 provides the pollutant reduction effects under the WCHP project using the DID model. As a weighted-average indicator of primary air pollutants, AQI can objectively represent the degree of air pollution [40].  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  had the highest percentage increment derived from scattered coal combustion during winter heating periods.  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{NO_x}$  were the main components of acid rain. Hence, we selected AQI,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , and  $\mathrm{NO_x}$  as explanatory variables. Results showed that AQI significantly increased (42.79) during periods when heating was employed, while the WCHP project decreased AQI by 8.72, offsetting approximately  $20.38\%$  of the negative impacts caused by winter heating. Likewise,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations decreased by  $18.59\%$  due to the WCHP project. This finding indicated that the WCHP project has had a significant reduction effect on air pollution during winter heating periods, particularly for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , since  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions decreased significantly by  $68.36\%$ . However, the reduction effect on  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  remained far from the percentage released by the official feedback report (i.e.,  $33.7\%$ ). pollutants in the western region were greater than that in the eastern region, except for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ . title: Fig. 4. Estimated coefficients (date) prior to and following the start time of heating.
references: As Fig. 4 shows, the 15 day estimator prior to and following the start of heating varied around a value of zero (0) devoid of a clear trend, and most coefficients were not statistically significant. This result indicated a similarity between pilot cities and non-pilot cities regarding AQI. Despite an abnormal increase 4-6 days following the start time of heating, such differences decreased to a negative value (as seen 2 days prior to the start time of heating), and this trend maintained for the ensuing two weeks. These results were consistent with the hypothesis that the WCHP project generated differential trends in pollutant concentrations between pilot and non-pilot cities. It is noteworthy that the period prior to the start time of heating was implemented and continued for 3-4 days in some cities prior to the formal start time of heating, which may have resulted in the early decrease. Additionally, we also estimated the Heatperiod coefficient 30 days following the start time of heating, which also indicated that effects of the WCHP project existed following the start time of heating.

where  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  is the set of dummy variables, which represents whether the  $i$ -th city is in a heating period at  $n-1$ -day.  $L_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days prior to the start time of heating, and  $E_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days following the start time of heating. With the omitted time category being the start time of heating,  $L_n$  captures the differences in pollutant indicators between pilot and non-pilot cities relative to differences just prior to the launch of the policy. Here we selected AQI as the air pollutant proxy. Under such specifications, if the signs of the estimated  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  coefficients differ, it can be assumed that changes in pollutant concentrations are not significant.

As a second robustness check, we constructed a counterfactual test to check whether our results could be driven by other unobserved factors. In doing so, we selected a different date during the non-heating period (May, June, July, August, September, and October) in lieu of the real start date of heating, specifying the interaction term between the treatment variable and the pseudoheating start time variable as Pseudo-HCP. The WCHP project can only take effect during the heating period, while the pseudo-HCP coefficient can reflect the impact of the WCHP project during the non-heating period. If estimated coefficients of pseudo-WCHP are significant and have the same sign as of the true WCHP shown in Table 4, we can conclude that air quality improvements in pilot cities could not be mainly attributed to the WCHP project, and the robustness of the DID model would be inadequate. In this case, improvements may be related to other pollution control policies, such as production restrictions and limits to the number of private vehicle allowed on roads.

According to the regressions shown in Table 5, only when the start time of heating was specified in October, coefficients of pseudo-WCHP were significantly negative at a  $1\%$  level, while other estimation results were not significant or had a positive sign. Compared to Table 4, these conflicting results failed to reject the validity of the "WHCD" policy. That is to say, the effect of the WCHP project was evident. In fact, we specified the heating start time on the 15th of November in consideration of the "official" start time of central heating while ignoring differences caused by scattered coal combustion. In October, some rural areas in the pilot and non-pilot cities began to burn scattered coal for heating, which could cause an evident increase in pollution emissions [47,48].

title: Fig. 1. Energy consumption of three subsectors of the passenger transport, 1978-2017. Data sources: NBS [3,4]; AQSIQ [48]; Huo et al. [49] and the authors' calculation.
references: With accelerating urbanization in China, passenger transport energy use has rapidly increased, from 28.78 Mtce in 1997 to 252.47 Mtce in 2017, with an annual average growth rate of  $11.47\%$  (Fig. 1). Energy use could thus be divided into three development stages. From 1978 to 1990, the intercity public passenger transport was the main traveling mode of passengers, as the urban and the private vehicle subsectors were undeveloped. From 1990 to 2002, energy consumption of the private passenger vehicles gradually caught up with that of the intercity subsector. From 2003 to 2017, the private subsector became the major source of the passenger transport energy use. The energy intensity of passenger transport decreased continually due to technological advances, and the energy intensity of the private passenger vehicles decreased rapidly from 143.68 Tce/Mp-km (ton coal equivalent/million passenger-kilometers) in 1997 to 51.38 Tce/Mp-km in 2017, due to the policy of fuel consumption limits for passenger cars [48]. This corresponds to a huge reduction in fuel consumption. Without this reduction in energy intensity, the fuel consumption in the private passenger transport sector would have increased nearly three times greater than it is today.

The effects of main driving factors on total passenger transport energy use are analyzed briefly. # Energy growth sources and future energy-saving potentials in passenger transportation sector in China

In China, energy use from passenger vehicles is increasing rapidly due to urbanization and income growth, but the contributions of transport service structure shift and energy efficiency improvement, especially future energy-saving directions are controversial and unclear because of the differences in the choices of traffic modes, which may greatly affect the analysis results and their interpretations. This study investigates the growth sources of energy use in the passenger transport sector during 1979-2017 and future energy-saving potentials by 2030 by re-examining statistical data. Results show that the transport service increase and transport structure shift led to the increase in energy use by  $665.25\%$  and  $337.22\%$ , but approximately  $14\%$  of the increased energy use was offset by energy efficiency improvements, and its effect is limited in public transportation sector, compared with private passenger vehicle sector. In public passenger transport sector, adjusting the service or volume structure may be a desirable option while in private passenger vehicle sector the policy focus is more on improving fuel efficiency and reducing average annual kilometers traveled per capita. Overall, there is still at least  $28.3\%$  of energy reduction potential by energy efficiency improvement before 2030 compared to 2017 baseline level. Potential evaluation showed that the current potential of transport service structure adjustment based on industrial planning is limited, which highlights the necessity of reform, involving promoting the sharing transportation, railway, subway and buses.

In the last two decades, the transport sector has become a major source of energy consumption worldwide. In China, transport energy use increased from 58.63 Mtce (million tons of coal equivalent) in 1995 to 421.91 Mtce in 2017, with an annual average growth rate of nearly  $10\%$ . Energy use from passenger transport dominates total transport energy use, accounting for nearly  $60\%$  in 2017. By 2030, more than 300 million new rural residents are expected to enter the cities in China, which will have a great impact on the passenger transport energy consumption. Passengers are relatively easily guided by policy measures to change their energy-using behaviors in their daily travel, thus leading to low-carbon

However, one of the key challenges is that the current energy statistics of passenger transportation are fragmented in China. The lack of comprehensive statistic specification causes that the data is scattered into different sectors in energy statistical system. The data does not properly cover metrics of interest for energy consumption analysis of passenger transportation. Part of the data is covered in the Transport, Storage and Post sector and Residential Consumption sector in China Energy Statistical Yearbook [3]. The former only includes the intercity public passenger transport and

Many studies have investigated energy consumption and emissions increase in transport sector [2,5,6]. The relationship between transport energy use and socio-economic factors has also been intensively discussed using econometric models [7-11]. Various options to save energy and reduce emissions in the transport sector have been evaluated for their reduction potential, from both the technological and behavioral perspectives [1,12,13]. In transport studies, bottom-up methodology and scenario analysis were often used to study future trends, mitigation options, and reduction potential [14,15]. In particular, road transport and passenger transport have always been the hot topics for evaluating energy consumption and emissions [16,17].

Either from an economy-wide or a sectoral perspective, the driving forces of increased energy consumption have been extensively investigated [18-20]. In the transportation sector, these studies could be summarized from two perspectives, namely, 1) the sectoral perspective, and 2) the vehicle-based perspective. From the sectoral perspective, changes in energy consumption and emission have been analyzed either for the whole transportation sector or its subsectors. Activity factors, namely Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth, population increase, and propensity to travel, were often found to be the main drivers of growing energy consumption [21-24], which is similar to the passenger transport subsector [25-27]. Other studies have focused more on micro sections of transportation. For example, on the vehicle level, the vehicle population, fuel mix, and vehicle fuel efficiency were

With accelerating urbanization in China, passenger transport energy use has rapidly increased, from 28.78 Mtce in 1997 to 252.47 Mtce in 2017, with an annual average growth rate of  $11.47\%$  (Fig. 1). Energy use could thus be divided into three development stages. From 1978 to 1990, the intercity public passenger transport was the main traveling mode of passengers, as the urban and the private vehicle subsectors were undeveloped. From 1990 to 2002, energy consumption of the private passenger vehicles gradually caught up with that of the intercity subsector. From 2003 to 2017, the private subsector became the major source of the passenger transport energy use. The energy intensity of passenger transport decreased continually due to technological advances, and the energy intensity of the private passenger vehicles decreased rapidly from 143.68 Tce/Mp-km (ton coal equivalent/million passenger-kilometers) in 1997 to 51.38 Tce/Mp-km in 2017, due to the policy of fuel consumption limits for passenger cars [48]. This corresponds to a huge reduction in fuel consumption. Without this reduction in energy intensity, the fuel consumption in the private passenger transport sector would have increased nearly three times greater than it is today.

(c) Modal energy intensity  
Fig. 7. Energy consumption and passenger volume share of three modes of the urban public passenger transport, 1978-2017. Data sources: China Statistical Yearbook [4]; China Urban Construction Statistical Yearbook [69]; Statistics bulletin of transportation sector development [64]; Fuel consumption limits for passenger cars, Fuel consumption evaluation methods and targets for passenger cars [48] and the authors' personal calculation.

The data includes socio-economic activity, transport structure and energy intensity. The socio-economic activity data includes the population, the number of households and the GDP, which are from the China Statistical Yearbook [4]; the transport-related activity data includes the passenger service of the intercity public passenger transport, the passenger volume of the urban public passenger transport and the vehicle population of the private passenger vehicles, which are from 1) Passenger Kilometers of the Transport, Postal and Telecommunication Services, and Software Industry, 2) Basic Statistics on Public Transportation in Cities by Region of the Urban, Rural and Regional Development, 3) Possession of Private Vehicles of the Transport, Postal and Telecommunication Services, and Software Industry in the China Statistical Yearbook [4]. The structure data consists of the service structure of the intercity public modes, the volume structure of the urban public modes and the vehicle type structure of the private subsector, which are calculated based on the above transport-related activity data. The intensity data includes the energy intensity of the three subsectors and the average annual mileage of the private passenger vehicles, which are from the Yearbook of China Integrated transport [63], the Statistics bulletin of transportation sector development [64], the Railway Statistics [65], and the Fuel consumption limits for passenger cars [48]. The average annual mileage of the private passenger vehicles is from the survey data in the FEEI model [49].

The GDP per capita effect was the dominant contributor to energy consumption growth and its contribution increased over time, but the contribution decreased during 1996-2000 (Figs. 5b) and 2008-2010 (Fig. 5c) due to the Asian financial crisis and the global financial crisis respectively. The shift of various modes of passenger transport (structure effect) contributed to the energy consumption decrease in the years leading up to 1990 because the passenger service share of civil aviation decreased (Fig. 5c). Since 1990, the structure effect turned from a negative value to a positive value. After 1990, the highway and civil aviation services were promoted vigorously by the State Council and Civil Aviation Administration of China [50,66]. Therefore, adjusting the transport structure in the intercity subsector, such as promoting the transfer form highway and civil aviation to railway, could be an effective way to save energy. The passenger service intensity effect provides an increasingly negative contribution to the increase in energy consumption during 1978-2017. The energy intensity of the railway, waterway, and civil aviation services decreased rapidly due to technological advancements and improved management.

The GDP per capita effect was the dominant contributor to energy consumption growth due to rapid economic development in China. The most prominent contribution appeared during 1991-1995 and 2006-2010, with an energy consumption increase of  $275\%$  and  $195\%$ , respectively (Fig. 6b). The passenger volume structure effect was another main driver of the energy consumption growth due to the shift in the dominant mode of transport. From the late 1980s to the 1990s, the taxi industry grew rapidly, owing to the policy of open business implemented by the State Council

The multiplication decomposition results in the three subsectors are shown in Fig. 9. Five influential factors on energy consumption can be observed and are quite similar in their effects in the intercity and urban passenger transport subsectors. The GDP per capita increase was always the dominant contributor to rises in energy consumption, but this effect was larger in the urban subsector (2.713). The passenger volume/service per GDP played a critical role in reducing energy consumption as opposed to energy intensity in the public transportation sector. In contrast, however, the private transport subsector showed that fuel efficiency improvement, decline of family size and average annual kilometers per capita offset the effect of vehicle stock growth per capita together.

This study contributes to understanding the underlying driving factors of energy growth and exploring energy efficiency improvement opportunities in passenger transport sector in China. First, a comprehensive picture of passenger transport energy consumption trends during 1979-2017 in China is given. Main drivers of energy growth are briefly identified through re-classifying the fragmented data statistics, which, to our knowledge, are unique and original. Second, the effects of the main driving forces of increased energy consumption are investigated firstly by using a hybrid decomposition method. Third, the future energy-saving potentials of different policy pathways until 2020 and 2030 are estimated and compared involving sharing transportation, structure adjustment, energy efficiency improvement, and urban car ownership control.

The results show that there are diversified contributors to energy consumption depending on the traffic modes utilized. On the whole, GDP growth per capita, motorization level and shifts in service structure mainly contribute to energy consumption increase in passenger transport sector during the period 1979-2017, and they cause more than 10 times increase in energy consumption, but the actual energy consumption increased just by 8.77 times because of the effect of energy efficiency improvement. For the intercity and urban public passenger transport, the GDP growth per capita, the service or volume structure change, and population growth strongly dominate the changes in energy consumption, and the effect of energy efficiency improvement is not very significant on a relative basis. In the private passenger vehicle sector, however, the situation is significantly different. Fuel efficiency improvement, decline of family size and average annual kilometers traveled per capita together offset the positive impacts from motorization level rise and vehicle type structure change. Especially, the fuel efficiency improvement has enormously contributed to energy conservation since 2010 in China.

Outlook analysis shows that by 2030 energy conservation potential in intercity public passenger transport sector is limited (16.6%), but there are still huge energy-saving potentials in urban public passenger transport sector (54.3%) and private passenger transport sector (34.3%). They could totally lead to 31.6% of energy consumption reduction in passenger transport sector by 2030.

In order to reduce energy use in passenger transport sector, multiple policy directions deserve attention. In China, the motorization level could be regulated by some mandatory policies, such as the vehicle license lottery in major cities, such as Beijing and Tianjin. In future, it is essential to give priority to public transport and sharing transport, such as the subway, bus, car-sharing, and bike-sharing. Particularly, first, sharing transportation deserves more attention in China because Chinese people are more likely to accept sharing and collective ideas, and there are less regulatory restrictions in China. Second, the energy intensity reduction targets up until 2020 need to be strictly enforced, especially for fuel efficiency standards for private passenger vehicles. Third, low-intensity transport modes, such as railway, waterway, rail transit, and mini vehicles, are strongly recommended to promote the low-carbon transition.

title: Fig. 4. Decomposition of the energy consumption change in the passenger transport sector. Note: the energy consumption changes in Fig. 4b are the annual changes rather than the cumulative change.  \Delta E_{act}  is the passenger activity effect;  \Delta E_{str}  is the transport structure effect;  \Delta E_{int}  is the energy intensity effect.
references: The decomposition results are shown in Fig. 4. The energy consumption of passenger transport increased from 28.78 Mtce in 1997 to 252.47 Mtce in 2017 (Fig. 4a). It is found that the passenger service increase (passenger activity effect) and the service structure adjustment (transport structure effect) are the main drivers of energy use increase, which contribute 191.47 Mtce (665.25% of the original energy consumption) and 97.06 Mtce (337.22%), respectively, to the increase in total energy consumption, while the improved energy efficiency resulted in energy conservation amounting to 40.29 Mtce (225.3% of the 1997 total energy consumption). Thus, the transport structure effect still plays an important role in increasing passenger transport energy use, which warrants more attention. Since the activity is hard to adjusted via policy instruments, transport structure adjustment should be the focus in the next-step policy formulation. An in-depth investigation is carried out below.

(a) decomposition results in the whole study period, 1978-2017  
(c) decomposition results in every year, 1978-2017  
Fig. 5. Decomposition of the energy consumption change in the intercity public passenger transport subsector. Note:  $\Delta E_{pop}$  is the population effect;  $\Delta E_{GP}$  is the GDP per capita effect;  $\Delta E_{TI}$  is the passenger service intensity effect;  $\Delta E_{str}$  is the passenger service structure effect;  $\Delta E_{int}$  is the service energy intensity effect.

(c) decomposition results in every year, 1991-2017  
Fig. 6. Decomposition of the energy consumption change in the urban public passenger transport subsector. Note:  $\Delta E_{pop}$  is the population effect;  $\Delta E_{GP}$  is the GDP per capita effect;  $\Delta E_{TI}$  is the passenger volume intensity effect;  $\Delta E_{str}$  is the passenger volume structure effect;  $\Delta E_{int}$  is the volume energy intensity effect.

(c) decomposition results in every year, 1997-2017  
Fig. 8. Decomposition of the energy consumption change in private passenger vehicle subsector. Note:  $\Delta E_{\mathrm{hou}}$  is the household effect;  $\Delta E_{PH}$  is the population per household effect;  $\Delta E_{SP}$  is the motorization level effect;  $\Delta E_{str}$  is the vehicle type structure effect;  $\Delta E_{VKT}$  is the residents' travel inclination effect;  $\Delta E_{int}$  is the fuel efficiency effect.

is the sum of the passenger activity effect  $(\Delta E_{act})$ , the transport structure effect  $(\Delta E_{str})$  and the energy intensity effect  $(\Delta E_{int})$ , using the LMDI (Logarithmic Mean Divisia Index) method [54].

The results show that there are diversified contributors to energy consumption depending on the traffic modes utilized. On the whole, GDP growth per capita, motorization level and shifts in service structure mainly contribute to energy consumption increase in passenger transport sector during the period 1979-2017, and they cause more than 10 times increase in energy consumption, but the actual energy consumption increased just by 8.77 times because of the effect of energy efficiency improvement. For the intercity and urban public passenger transport, the GDP growth per capita, the service or volume structure change, and population growth strongly dominate the changes in energy consumption, and the effect of energy efficiency improvement is not very significant on a relative basis. In the private passenger vehicle sector, however, the situation is significantly different. Fuel efficiency improvement, decline of family size and average annual kilometers traveled per capita together offset the positive impacts from motorization level rise and vehicle type structure change. Especially, the fuel efficiency improvement has enormously contributed to energy conservation since 2010 in China.
concise_summary: Fig. 4 shows decomposition of energy consumption changes in passenger transport (1997-2017) highlighting effects of passenger activity, transport structure, and energy intensity.
inferred_insight: The transport structure and passenger activity significantly drive energy consumption increase, outweighing improvements in energy efficiency. Policy should prioritize transport structure adjustment.
