title: Fig. 4. Normalized average diurnal variations in  O_3  and typical VOCs during the dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019 (shaded part:  \pm SD ).
references: The average diurnal variations in mixing ratios of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and selected VOCs during the dry and wet seasons are shown in Fig. 4. To more clearly compare the diurnal variation trends of the pollutants, all concentrations were normalized (divided by their respective average concentrations). The diurnal variations of the same species during the dry and wet seasons were similar, but  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and OVOCs rose relatively more during the daytime during the warmer wet season. The diurnal variation of benzene showed two peaks corresponding to the morning and afternoon traffic emissions, but the concentration decreased in the afternoon, which may

Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.

A total of 17 species of VOCs were monitored in this study by PTR-MS from 2014 to 2019, including 6 species classified as OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, formic acid, acetic acid, and MEK); 5 aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene, styrene, C8 and C9 aromatics); 3 classified as biogenic species (isoprene, MVK + MACR, and monoterpenes); and 2 species that, in addition to benzene, were measured as tracers (acetonitrile and DMS). The compounds, the respective  $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{z}$  of their parent ion signal, and their detection limits are provided in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 2, the average of the sum of the VOC concentrations in the dry season over the six years ranged from 25.3 to 41.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 33.1 ppbv; for the wet season, the average value ranged from 23.0 to 39.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 29.4 ppbv. In terms of the proportion of each component, in the six years of dry and wet seasons, OVOCs (70–78%) accounted for the largest proportion, followed by aromatic hydrocarbons (16–26%), whereas the concentrations of biogenic VOCs (BVOCs) and tracers were relatively low (less than 10%). Figure S6 shows the time series of 17 species of VOCs during the sampling period. To evaluate the concentrations of OVOCs in this study, comparative results of the concentrations of six OVOCs in five urban areas in China are listed in Table 1. Compared to Guangzhou and Hong Kong, two other mega-cities in the PRD region, the concentrations of acetaldehyde, acetone, and MEK in Shenzhen were higher than those in Hong

In this study, the six-year average of VOCs in the dry season was approximately  $13\%$  higher than that in the wet season. As shown in Figure S7, the concentrations of OVCs, aromatic hydrocarbons and tracers in the wet season were lower than those in the dry season, while the BVOC concentration was higher in the wet season. As a highly developed urban area, Shenzhen was active in anthropogenic activities year-round, and there was no obvious seasonal difference in anthropogenic emissions. Therefore, it is more likely that seasonal meteorological differences lead to variations in VOC concentrations between wet and dry seasons. For example, the air masses passing through Shenzhen during the dry season were predominantly from northern inland regions, and the concentration of pollutants carried by inland air masses was higher than that of marine air masses (He et al., 2011; Huang et al., 2020). In addition, the dry season had a lower boundary layer and less precipitation, which was also conducive to the accumulation of pollutants. However, the concentration of BVOCs during the wet season was significantly higher than during the dry season, as shown in Figure S7 (g), (h) and (i), primarily because the higher light levels and solar radiation during the wet season led to accelerated release rates of VOCs by plants (Loreto et al., 2010). It should be pointed out that the data collection frequency in the dry season  $(\sim 73\%)$  was much higher than that in the wet season  $(\sim 35\%)$ , which may lead to relatively higher uncertainty of the conclusion for the wet season.

These results suggested that photochemical production and/or daytime anthropogenic activities related to OVC emissions were notable and could overwhelm photochemical loss. The diurnal variation trend of acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O_3}$ , exhibiting a greater difference in concentration between day and night during the wet season, indicating that the majority of acetaldehyde originated from daytime secondary generation (Chang et al., 2019). The daytime splits of the diurnal curves of methanol, MEK, and acetic acid, similar to benzene, implied that they could have relatively larger contributions from primary emissions sources, such as motor vehicle emissions. (Guo et al., 2011, Zheng et al., 2013). The diurnal variations in VOCs in our previous two individual campaigns, which were carried out during the dry season (Huang et al., 2019, 2020), were almost the same as those in the dry season in this study.
Fig. 5. The average mixing ratios of the measured VOCs and calculated OFPs in the dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.

PTR-MS was used for continuous online monitoring of atmospheric VOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019. The results revealed that OVOCs were the most abundant of 17 species of VOCs measured, comprising  $70 - 78\%$ . The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\mathrm{ppb}$ . There were diurnal variations in OVOC concentrations, with higher concentrations during the daytime. The diurnal variation in acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O}_3$ , and it had the largest OFP of the OVOC species, indicating that acetaldehyde was an important intermediate of photochemical reactions. The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA.
concise_summary: Normalized diurnal variations of O3 and VOCs during dry and wet seasons from 2014-2019 show distinct daytime peaks, with higher concentrations in the wet season due to photochemical processes.
inferred_insight: The figure highlights the influence of seasonal photochemical processes on VOC concentrations, demonstrating effective primary pollution control while suggesting more efforts for secondary sources.

title: Fig. 5. The average mixing ratios of the measured VOCs and calculated OFPs in the dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.
references: concentration to obtain OFP (ppbv). Fig. 5 shows the average concentration and OFP of each VOC in the dry and wet seasons over six years. The top three species contributing to OFP in the dry and wet seasons were acetaldehyde, methanol and toluene, which accounted for  $83\%$  and  $78\%$  of the OFP contributed by all species in the dry and wet seasons, respectively. Methanol made a significant contribution to OFP because its atmospheric concentration was the highest among all VOCs, while acetaldehyde and toluene made significant contributions because of their high reactivity. In addition, the contribution of isoprene to OFP was not negligible and was significantly higher in the wet season than in the dry season, which also reflects the increased contribution of biogenic sources in the wet season. Compared with the study of the VOC emission inventory in the PRD (Zheng et al., 2009; Lyu et al., 2020), the contribution of OVOCs in this study was more significant because the emission inventory study usually evaluates only the primary emission source, while the secondary source of OVOCs, such as acetaldehyde, cannot be ignored. Aldehydes not only have high reactivity but also can generate OH radicals by photolysis (Volkamer et al., 2010; Xue et al., 2016). Therefore, aldehydes are important products and intermediates of photochemistry reactions, and they are also the key VOC species for subsequent research on atmospheric oxidation capacity.

Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.

The average diurnal variations in mixing ratios of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and selected VOCs during the dry and wet seasons are shown in Fig. 4. To more clearly compare the diurnal variation trends of the pollutants, all concentrations were normalized (divided by their respective average concentrations). The diurnal variations of the same species during the dry and wet seasons were similar, but  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and OVOCs rose relatively more during the daytime during the warmer wet season. The parameterization method based on photochemical aging described in section 2.4 was used to analyze the sources of OVOCs during the daytime (8:00-20:00). Referencing the source analysis results for the OVOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019 (in Table 2), the measured concentrations of six OVOC species monitored by PTR-MS were in good agreement with the simulated concentrations, and the measured values of each OVOC species were highly correlated with the simulated values (R was between 0.71 and 0.86, except for formic acid, which was 0.62). For the six-year average, the sources of methanol and MEK were similar, with the largest contribution from primary anthropogenic sources  $(47 - 51\%)$  followed by biogenic and background sources. The contributions to acetaldehyde and acetic acid from primary and secondary anthropogenic sources were similar  $(26 - 32\%)$  followed by biogenic sources (approximately  $20\%$ ). title: Fig. 6. Six-year trends of the contributions from different sources to daytime OVOC concentrations.
references: Fig. 6 shows the trends in the relative contributions to OVOCs from 2014 to 2019 during the dry and wet seasons. In general, during the six years from 2014 to 2019, primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to OVOCs during Shenzhen's dry season, but the proportion exhibited a decreasing trend year over year, and the proportion during the six-year period decreased by approximately  $15\%$  overall. This continuous decreasing trend indicates that the VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas, where most of the industry and traffic of Shenzhen is located, had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. The OVOC source compositions during the wet season, when clean marine air masses from the south dominated the sampling site, did not exhibit a clear year-by-year trend. The contributions of secondary anthropogenic sources to OVOC concentrations (except that of methanol) during the wet season were significantly higher (approximately 1.3-1.6 times greater) than those during the dry season, indicating that secondary transformation of NMHCs during the wet season contributed greatly to OVOC concentrations. Biogenic sources reasonably contributed a larger proportion of OVOC concentrations during the wet season, whereas biomass burning contributed a larger fraction of acetaldehyde during the dry season.

As presented in Fig. 1, Shenzhen is located in the PRD of Guangdong Province, China. The sampling site for this study was located on the campus of the Shenzhen Graduate School of Peking University (PKUSZ,  $22.60^{\circ}\mathrm{N}$ ,  $113.97^{\circ}\mathrm{E}$ ) in western Shenzhen, which is predominantly surrounded by green areas, teaching buildings and apartments. No evident anthropogenic emission sources of air pollutants surrounded the sampling site, except a highway approximately  $100\mathrm{m}$  away. Previous studies have shown that the concentrations of air pollutants such as  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and VOCs at this site were approximately equal to their average concentrations in the entire PRD region (Louie et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2018). Therefore, this site was considered suitable for long-term observation of trends in air pollutants in the PRD region. Table S1 summarizes the effective days of VOCs observed in each month from 2014 to 2019, with 1169 days in total over the six years of sampling. During the six-year sampling period, the loss of data was mainly due to instrument failure and bad weather, especially in the wet season due to frequent rainfall, resulting in a higher rate of missing data during

Shenzhen belongs to the subtropical marine monsoon climate zone. Marked differences in the four seasons were not evident, and the observation time each year was slightly different; therefore, this study divided the entire year into a dry season and a wet season according to the monthly temperature and precipitation. As presented in Figure S1, the period from May to October, with higher temperatures and abundant precipitation, was defined as the wet season, and the period from November to April, with less precipitation, was defined as the dry season. The meteorological data are from the Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality (Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). In addition to the differences in temperature and precipitation, the sources of air masses in Shenzhen during the wet and dry seasons were also very different. Taking January and July 2018 as an example, the 48-h backward trajectories at  $100\mathrm{m}$  above ground level in Shenzhen  $(113.9^{\circ}\mathrm{E}, 22.6^{\circ}\mathrm{N})$  were calculated beginning at 12:00 UTC using the Hybrid Single Particle Lagrangian Integrated Trajectory (HYSPLIT) model (Draxler and Rolph, 2003). The results show that the origin of the air mass passing through Shenzhen in different seasons was very different: during the dry season, the air mass mainly came from the northern mainland, and during the wet season, it mainly came from the South China Sea, which has also been explained in detail in our previous studies (Zou et al., 2017). In addition, referencing existing studies of air pollution in the Pearl River Delta, many scholars have adopted a method of dividing the wet and dry seasons (Cui et al., 2015). Figure S2 shows the changes in major meteorological parameters during the six-year observation period in Shenzhen. Analysis of major meteorological parameters in Shenzhen (Fig S2) did not indicate significant interannual variability in temperature, rainfall, humidity or wind conditions that could have substantially influenced the long-term variation in atmospheric pollutants observed in this study.

It should be emphasized that because the sampling data of this study span six years, in addition to the above assumptions, one more assumption should be added: (4) during the sampling period, there was no significant change in the ratio of OVOC anthropogenic emissions to tracers (benzene) in Shenzhen. The emission reduction strategy of Shenzhen is to reduce emission sources of pollutants as a whole, such as replacing gasoline vehicles with electric vehicles and closing down or moving VOC-emitting enterprises to other areas. These measures have reduced emissions of VOCs, but the emission ratio among specific VOC species is relatively stable. Toluene/benzene (T/B) was considered a good indicator to judge the contribution of motor vehicle sources and solvent use to VOC emissions (Nelson and Quigley, 1984; Wang et al., 2002; Araizaga et al., 2013). Figure S5 shows the changes in the concentrations of benzene and toluene and T/B from 2014 to 2019. The concentrations of benzene and toluene in the six years showed a downward trend, while T/B remained at approximately 5, which also indicated

Fig. 7 presents the changes in absolute concentrations of OVOCs contributed by different sources during the dry seasons for the six-year study period. To better depict the contribution trends of different OVOC sources, the parameters were normalized (based on

the OVOC concentrations in 2014). From 2014 to 2019, the ambient concentration of OVOCs contributed by primary anthropogenic sources decreased significantly, declining by  $50 - 60\%$ . Among the six OVOC species, the species with the largest decrease in concentration attributed to primary anthropogenic sources was methanol, which decreased by 7 ppb. However, contributions of the other sources to the absolute concentrations of OVOCs did not significantly change over the six-year period. Specifically, annual variations in secondary anthropogenic sources were not significant, indicating that secondary formation of atmospheric OVOCs in this region might be restricted by the atmospheric oxidizing capacity and that precursor emissions are more than abundant.

VOC emission reduction in industrial enterprises and the coating industry occurs mainly through upgrading emission standards and closing down industries with high VOC emissions. For example, furniture manufacturing used to be an important industry in Shenzhen but also a key VOC-emitting industry. From 2010 to 2016, 565 coating production lines were removed or relocated in Shenzhen, reducing VOC emissions by more than 30,000 tons every year. Since 2017, Shenzhen has completely banned solvent paints and adhesives in the manufacturing process and replaced them with water-based paints to further reduce VOC emissions, especially the emissions of aldehydes and ketones commonly used in the production of furniture manufacturing (Ecological Environment Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). As a commonly used tracer of anthropogenic sources of VOCs, the annual average concentration of benzene during the dry season from 2014 to 2019 was 0.98, 0.81, 0.64, 0.61, 0.49, and 0.45 ppb (Figure S5), respectively, reflecting the decreasing trend in primary VOC emissions in recent years. Therefore, the decrease in OVOC concentrations during the dry season over the six-year study period could primarily be attributed to decreases in the contributions from primary anthropogenic sources. title: Fig. 7. Change in ambient concentrations of OVOCs contributed by different sources during the dry seasons of 2014-2019.
references: Fig. 7 presents the changes in absolute concentrations of OVOCs contributed by different sources during the dry seasons for the six-year study period. To better depict the contribution trends of different OVOC sources, the parameters were normalized (based on

Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.

Previous studies have proven that some low-carbon OVOCs (formaldehyde and acetaldehyde) can generate OH radicals and hydroperoxyl radicals  $\mathrm{(HO_2)}$  through photolysis to affect the oxidizing capacity of the atmosphere (Volkamer et al., 2010; Shao et al., 2011; Xue et al., 2016). OVOCs can also participate in photochemical reactions and contribute to the generation of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and SOA (Louie et al., 2013; Ehn et al., 2014). In addition, as most OVOCs are highly water soluble, they can be absorbed into the human body, irritating the eyes, skin and respiratory tract, thus harming human health. OVOCs such as formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, acetone and acrolein have been shown to pose serious health risks (WHO, 2000, Delfino et al., 2003; Bashkin, 2009; Du et al., 2014). Given the important role of OVOCs in photochemical reactions and the complexity of their sources, OVOCs have been the subject of extensive research recently (Sahu et al., 2017; Maji et al., 2020), especially in China, where air pollution is severe (Zhang et al., 2012, 2015; Zhou et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). Zhang et al. (2012) studied the pollution characteristics of BTEX (benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, m,p-xylene and o-xylene) and carbonyl compounds during summer in Beijing and found that the  $\mathrm{O_3}$  formation potential of carbonyl compounds  $(166.1~\mu \mathrm{g} / \mathrm{m}^3)$  was approximately 2.5 times that of BTEX  $(65.4~\mu \mathrm{g} / \mathrm{m}^3)$ . Zhou et al. (2019) used proton transfer reaction mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) to measure VOCs in Xi'an and used the positive matrix factorization (PMF) model to analyze over 30 types of VOCs and identify eight primary sources and found that local emissions, such as industrial processes, solvent usage, residential biofuel use and vehicular exhaust, contributed about  $70\%$  of the VOCs measured. Zhu et al. (2019) used PTR-MS to monitor OVOCs during different seasons and applied photochemical age parameter analysis to evaluate the sources of OVOCs in Shenzhen. They found that primary anthropogenic sources were the most important source of OVOCs in urban areas of China.

Shenzhen belongs to the subtropical marine monsoon climate zone. Marked differences in the four seasons were not evident, and the observation time each year was slightly different; therefore, this study divided the entire year into a dry season and a wet season according to the monthly temperature and precipitation. As presented in Figure S1, the period from May to October, with higher temperatures and abundant precipitation, was defined as the wet season, and the period from November to April, with less precipitation, was defined as the dry season. The meteorological data are from the Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality (Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). In addition to the differences in temperature and precipitation, the sources of air masses in Shenzhen during the wet and dry seasons were also very different. Taking January and July 2018 as an example, the 48-h backward trajectories at  $100\mathrm{m}$  above ground level in Shenzhen  $(113.9^{\circ}\mathrm{E}, 22.6^{\circ}\mathrm{N})$  were calculated beginning at 12:00 UTC using the Hybrid Single Particle Lagrangian Integrated Trajectory (HYSPLIT) model (Draxler and Rolph, 2003). The results show that the origin of the air mass passing through Shenzhen in different seasons was very different: during the dry season, the air mass mainly came from the northern mainland, and during the wet season, it mainly came from the South China Sea, which has also been explained in detail in our previous studies (Zou et al., 2017). In addition, referencing existing studies of air pollution in the Pearl River Delta, many scholars have adopted a method of dividing the wet and dry seasons (Cui et al., 2015). Figure S2 shows the changes in major meteorological parameters during the six-year observation period in Shenzhen. Analysis of major meteorological parameters in Shenzhen (Fig S2) did not indicate significant interannual variability in temperature, rainfall, humidity or wind conditions that could have substantially influenced the long-term variation in atmospheric pollutants observed in this study.

Generally, CO (de Gouw et al., 2005, 2018), acetylene (Yuan et al., 2012) and benzene (Ma et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019) are commonly used tracers representing anthropogenic sources of VOCs. Anthropogenic emissions of VOCs in China are rather complicated, with industrial solvents and motor vehicle exhaust contributing significantly to VOCs. However, CO and acetylene mainly come from combustion emissions, which cannot represent well the anthropogenic emissions of VOCs in China. Compared with CO and acetylene, benzene can better reflect the anthropogenic emissions of VOCs in the PRD. For example, Zhang et al. (2013) used the PMF model to analyze the source of VOCs in the PRD and found that combustion processes and industrial emissions were the predominant sources of benzene. Second, considering the photolysis of aldehydes and ketones during the daytime, which obviously conflicts with assumption (2), we need to consider the effect of the photodegradation of aldehydes and ketones on their removal. Referring to the studies of Wang et al. (2017) and Huang et al. (2020), we introduced a coefficient  $(\mathrm{m})$  before the OH reaction rate constant  $\left(\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{ovoc}}\right)$  to compensate for the daytime loss of aldehydes and ketones caused by photolysis. A detailed description of the calculation method of the m value is given in the supporting information. For methanol, formic acid and acetic acid, with almost no photolysis (Saunders et al., 2003), this study ignored the effect of photolysis on daytime removal (set m as 1), while the calculated m values for acetaldehyde, acetone and methyl ethyl ketone (MEK) were 1.02, 1.26 and 1.25, respectively, during the sampling period. Third, previous studies have shown that biomass burning emissions are an inevitable source of OVOCs (Yuan et al., 2010; Warneke et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2016). For example, Yuan et al. (2010) reported that biomass burning emissions in the PRD contributed  $9.5 - 17.7\%$  to nine kinds of VOCs (including methanol, acetone, and MEK), suggesting that the contribution of biomass burning to OVOCs cannot be ignored. Therefore, the last parameter  $(ER_{bb} * [\text{Tracer}_{bb}])$  in Equation (1) was added in this study to estimate the contribution of biomass burning to OVOCs.  $[\text{Tracer}_{bb}]$  represents the concentration of ambient acetonitrile, which is an excellent indicator for biomass burning and can be accurately measured by PTR-MS (de Gouw et al., 2003b). In addition, previous studies have shown that benzene mainly comes from transportation and solvent use in the PRD, and biomass burning accounts for less than  $10\%$  (Lyu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2013), so the influence of using benzene as a tracer of anthropogenic sources on biomass burning emissions can be ignored.  $\mathrm{ER}_{bb}$  is the emission factor of OVOCs relative to acetonitrile, which was calculated by least-squares fit along with  $\mathrm{ER}_{\mathrm{OVOC}}$ ,  $\mathrm{ER}_{\mathrm{precursor}}$ ,  $\mathrm{ER}_{\mathrm{biogen}}$ ,  $\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{precursor}}$ , and [background].

It should be emphasized that because the sampling data of this study span six years, in addition to the above assumptions, one more assumption should be added: (4) during the sampling period, there was no significant change in the ratio of OVOC anthropogenic emissions to tracers (benzene) in Shenzhen. The emission reduction strategy of Shenzhen is to reduce emission sources of pollutants as a whole, such as replacing gasoline vehicles with electric vehicles and closing down or moving VOC-emitting enterprises to other areas. These measures have reduced emissions of VOCs, but the emission ratio among specific VOC species is relatively stable. Toluene/benzene (T/B) was considered a good indicator to judge the contribution of motor vehicle sources and solvent use to VOC emissions (Nelson and Quigley, 1984; Wang et al., 2002; Araizaga et al., 2013). Figure S5 shows the changes in the concentrations of benzene and toluene and T/B from 2014 to 2019. The concentrations of benzene and toluene in the six years showed a downward trend, while T/B remained at approximately 5, which also indicated

A total of 17 species of VOCs were monitored in this study by PTR-MS from 2014 to 2019, including 6 species classified as OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, formic acid, acetic acid, and MEK); 5 aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene, styrene, C8 and C9 aromatics); 3 classified as biogenic species (isoprene, MVK + MACR, and monoterpenes); and 2 species that, in addition to benzene, were measured as tracers (acetonitrile and DMS). The compounds, the respective  $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{z}$  of their parent ion signal, and their detection limits are provided in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 2, the average of the sum of the VOC concentrations in the dry season over the six years ranged from 25.3 to 41.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 33.1 ppbv; for the wet season, the average value ranged from 23.0 to 39.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 29.4 ppbv. In terms of the proportion of each component, in the six years of dry and wet seasons, OVOCs (70–78%) accounted for the largest proportion, followed by aromatic hydrocarbons (16–26%), whereas the concentrations of biogenic VOCs (BVOCs) and tracers were relatively low (less than 10%). Figure S6 shows the time series of 17 species of VOCs during the sampling period. To evaluate the concentrations of OVOCs in this study, comparative results of the concentrations of six OVOCs in five urban areas in China are listed in Table 1. Compared to Guangzhou and Hong Kong, two other mega-cities in the PRD region, the concentrations of acetaldehyde, acetone, and MEK in Shenzhen were higher than those in Hong

The parameterization method based on photochemical aging described in section 2.4 was used to analyze the sources of OVOCs during the daytime (8:00-20:00). Referencing the source analysis results for the OVOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019 (in Table 2), the measured concentrations of six OVOC species monitored by PTR-MS were in good agreement with the simulated concentrations, and the measured values of each OVOC species were highly correlated with the simulated values (R was between 0.71 and 0.86, except for formic acid, which was 0.62). For the six-year average, the sources of methanol and MEK were similar, with the largest contribution from primary anthropogenic sources  $(47 - 51\%)$  followed by biogenic and background sources. The contributions to acetaldehyde and acetic acid from primary and secondary anthropogenic sources were similar  $(26 - 32\%)$  followed by biogenic sources (approximately  $20\%$ ). In contrast to other OVOCs, acetone was the most affected by regional background concentration  $(37\%)$  which may be because acetone is relatively inactive and widely exists in the atmosphere, and its ground-level background mixing ratios can reach 500 pptv throughout the troposphere under extremely clean conditions (Seinfeld and Pandis, 2016). For formic acid, secondary anthropogenic sources contributed the most  $(34\%)$

Fig. 6 shows the trends in the relative contributions to OVOCs from 2014 to 2019 during the dry and wet seasons. In general, during the six years from 2014 to 2019, primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to OVOCs during Shenzhen's dry season, but the proportion exhibited a decreasing trend year over year, and the proportion during the six-year period decreased by approximately  $15\%$  overall. This continuous decreasing trend indicates that the VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas, where most of the industry and traffic of Shenzhen is located, had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. The OVOC source compositions during the wet season, when clean marine air masses from the south dominated the sampling site, did not exhibit a clear year-by-year trend. The contributions of secondary anthropogenic sources to OVOC concentrations (except that of methanol) during the wet season were significantly higher (approximately 1.3-1.6 times greater) than those during the dry season, indicating that secondary transformation of NMHCs during the wet season contributed greatly to OVOC concentrations. Biogenic sources reasonably contributed a larger proportion of OVOC concentrations during the wet season, whereas biomass burning contributed a larger fraction of acetaldehyde during the dry season.

Among the six OVOC species, the species with the largest decrease in concentration attributed to primary anthropogenic sources was methanol, which decreased by 7 ppb. However, contributions of the other sources to the absolute concentrations of OVOCs did not significantly change over the six-year period. Specifically, annual variations in secondary anthropogenic sources were not significant, indicating that secondary formation of atmospheric OVOCs in this region might be restricted by the atmospheric oxidizing capacity and that precursor emissions are more than abundant.

VOC emission reduction in industrial enterprises and the coating industry occurs mainly through upgrading emission standards and closing down industries with high VOC emissions. For example, furniture manufacturing used to be an important industry in Shenzhen but also a key VOC-emitting industry. From 2010 to 2016, 565 coating production lines were removed or relocated in Shenzhen, reducing VOC emissions by more than 30,000 tons every year. Since 2017, Shenzhen has completely banned solvent paints and adhesives in the manufacturing process and replaced them with water-based paints to further reduce VOC emissions, especially the emissions of aldehydes and ketones commonly used in the production of furniture manufacturing (Ecological Environment Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). As a commonly used tracer of anthropogenic sources of VOCs, the annual average concentration of benzene during the dry season from 2014 to 2019 was 0.98, 0.81, 0.64, 0.61, 0.49, and 0.45 ppb (Figure S5), respectively, reflecting the decreasing trend in primary VOC emissions in recent years. Therefore, the decrease in OVOC concentrations during the dry season over the six-year study period could primarily be attributed to decreases in the contributions from primary anthropogenic sources. PTR-MS was used for continuous online monitoring of atmospheric VOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019. The results revealed that OVOCs were the most abundant of 17 species of VOCs measured, comprising  $70 - 78\%$ . The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\mathrm{ppb}$ . There were diurnal variations in OVOC concentrations, with higher concentrations during the daytime. The diurnal variation in acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O}_3$ , and it had the largest OFP of the OVOC species, indicating that acetaldehyde was an important intermediate of photochemical reactions. The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA.

title: Figure 1. Domain and component of the MIX emission inventory.
references: Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.
# MIX: a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission inventory under the international collaboration framework of the MICS-Asia and HTAP

Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$

In order to support the MICS-Asia III and other global and regional modeling activities with the best available anthropogenic emission dataset over Asia, we develop a new Asian anthropogenic emission inventory, named MIX, by harmonizing different local emission inventories with the mosaic approach. The mosaic inventory developed in this work will provide (1) a more complete and state-of-the-art understanding of anthropogenic emissions over Asia with best estimates from local inventories; (2) a reference dataset with moderate accuracy and resolution that can support both scientific research and mitigation policy-making; and (3) broader application of the best available local inventories in modeling studies by processing them to model-ready format and including them in a publicly available emission dataset.

The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated

This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in
# 2 Compilation of the MIX emission inventory

We then selected different emission datasets for various species for each country by the following hierarchy. REAS2 was used as the default where local emission data are absent. Emission inventories compiled by the official agencies or developed with more local information are selected to override REAS2, which include MEIC for mainland China, ANL-India for India, and CAPSS for the Republic of Korea. Detailed information and advantages of these inventories are presented in Sect. 2.2. As only a few species  $(\mathrm{SO}_2,$  BC, OC, and power plant  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  were available from ANL-India, REAS2 was used to supplement the missing species. A mosaic process was then used to combine ANL-India and REAS2 into a single dataset for Indian emissions. It is worth noting that the REAS2 has incorporated local inventories for Japan and Taiwan, which are subsequently adopted in MIX for these two regions. PKU-NH<sub>3</sub> was further used to replace MEIC emissions for NH<sub>3</sub> over China, given that PKU-NH<sub>3</sub> was developed with a process-based model that represented the spatiotemporal variations in NH<sub>3</sub> emissions. Table 2 lists the information of each inventory used in MIX.

Figure 2 illustrates the mosaic process for the MIX inventory development. Each dataset was reprocessed to  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution with monthly variations when necessary. We used monthly gridded emissions from each component inventory where available and assumed no monthly variation in emissions when the component inventory only provided annual emissions. The monthly profiles and spatial proxies used in each component emission inventories are summarized in Tables S3 and S4.
Table 1. Summary of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory.
Figure 2. Schematic methodology of the MIX emission inventory development.
# 2.2 Components of the MIX emission inventory

The REAS2 inventory is provided with monthly gridded emission data for both air pollutants and  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  by sectors at  $0.25\times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. We aggregated the 11 REAS2 sectors to 5 sectors provided in the MIX inventory. Emissions from open biomass burning, aviation, and international shipping were excluded from the REAS2 before incorporating into MIX. Monthly variations are developed for power plants, industry, residential sources, and cold-start emissions from vehicles by various monthly profiles (Kurokawa et al., 2013). In REAS2, power plants with annual  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions larger than  $1\mathrm{Tg}$  were provided as point sources with coordinates of locations, while emissions for other sectors were processed as areal sources and gridded at  $0.25\times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution using maps of rural, urban, and total populations and road networks (see Table S4).

A high-resolution Indian emission inventory developed by ANL (referred to as ANL-India hereafter; Lu et al., 2011) was used in the MIX inventory. ANL-India used a technology-based methodology to estimate  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC emissions in India for the period of 1996-2010. Major anthropogenic sources including both fossil-fuel and biofuel combustion are covered in ANL-India. Time-dependent trends in emission factors were developed by taking account of the impact of technology changes on emissions (Habib et al., 2004; Venkataraman et al., 2005). Lu and Streets (2012) further updated power plant emissions in India by calculating emissions at the generating unit level ( $\sim$ 800 units in total) based on information from the reports of the Central Electricity Authority (CEA), including geographical location, capacity, fuel type, electricity generation, time the plant was commissioned/decommissioned, etc. The exact location of each power plant was obtained from the Global Energy Observatory (http://globalenergyobservatory.org) and crosschecked through Google Earth. The updated unit-based power plant emissions in ANL-India are available for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , BC, and OC.

For the Republic of Korea, we used the CAPSS emission inventory developed by the National Institute of Environmental Research of Korea (Lee et al., 2011). CAPSS estimated emissions with four levels of source classifications. We mapped emissions from 12 first-level aggregated source categories (SCC1) to five sectors in MIX. The CAPSS inventory included emissions for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and five regulated air pollutants,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC, and  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ . We derived sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and the other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011) and applied those

ratios to estimate  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions. In the MIX inventory, we used the 2008 and 2009 CAPSS inventories to represent 2008 and 2010 emissions of the Republic of Korea, because 2009 is the most recent year of CAPSS inventory at the time the MIX inventory was developed. In the CAPSS inventory, point sources, area sources, and mobile sources were processed using different spatial allocation approaches (Lee et al., 2011). We used the  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  emission product from CAPSS as input for the MIX inventory. Only annual total emissions were presented in the CAPSS inventory. In the MIX inventory, we assume no monthly variation in emissions in the Republic of Korea.

We directly used monthly emissions from each regional emission inventory when compiling the MIX inventory. We assume no monthly variation in emissions when monthly profiles are absent from the regional emission inventories. Table S3 presents the monthly profiles used in each component emission inventory for MIX. In summary, monthly profiles for power plant emissions usually developed based on monthly statistics of power generation. Monthly profiles of industrial emissions are derived from monthly output of industrial products or industrial GDP. Residential monthly profiles are estimated from stove operation time based on ambient temperatures by regions (Streets et al., 2003).

Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.
Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.

Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use

In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.

MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in
A comprehensive intercomparison among different emission inventories over Asia was conducted by Kurokawa et al. (2013). In this work, we compare the MIX inventory

with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi
Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.

ences by region and by sector. Regionally, the differences can be largely attributed to disagreements in emission estimates for China and India, as presented in Table 7. Discrepancies are relatively large at the sector level compared to total emissions. EDGAR's estimates for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions from power plants are  $60\%$  higher than estimates in MIX. For China,  $70\%$  of power generation capacities were equipped with FGD and the average  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  removal efficiency was  $78\%$  (Liu et al., 2015). The high estimates in EDGAR v4.2 are most likely due to underestimation of FGD penetration or  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  removal efficiencies of FGD (Kurokawa et al., 2013). Remarkable differences for the residential and transportation sectors are found for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, and NMVOC estimates in the two inventories. For instance, EDGAR v4.2 estimates lower  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions of transportation sector by 27 and by  $48\%$  for the residential sector compared to MIX. Similarly, residential CO emissions in EDGAR v4.2 are  $33\%$  lower than in MIX, leading to  $33\%$  lower estimates of CO emissions in EDGAR v4.2 compared to MIX. Underestimates of CO emissions in EDGAR v4.2 inventory have been confirmed by top-down constraints (Pétron et al., 2004; Fortems-Cheiney et al., 2011). As the statistical differences of energy use are usually within  $30\%$  at sector level (Guan et al., 2012), the discrepancy by sector could only be attributed to differences in the raw emission factors and abatement measures. Al-

+13.6% compared to MIX) but lower emissions for industry  $(-293\mathrm{Tg}, -6.8\%)$  emissions. EDGAR v4.2 estimates lower  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions for China  $(-308\mathrm{Tg}, -3.4\%)$  and higher emissions for other regions: +102Tg (+5.6%) for Other East Asia, +83.9Tg (+5.6%) for Southeast Asia, +204Tg (+9.7%) for India, +29.5Tg (+7.5%) for Other South Asia, and +25.3Tg (+6.4%) for Central Asia. At sector level, EDGAR v4.2 estimates are 29% (+833 Tg) higher for power, 22% (-944Tg) lower for industry, and 31% (-192 Tg) lower for transportation over China compared to MIX. For Other East Asia, differences between EDGAR v4.2 and MIX are mainly contributed by power sector, with 20% higher emissions (+130 Tg) in EDGAR v4.2. Residential sector is the main contributor to the differences between EDGAR v4.2 and MIX over India, with 28% (+177 Tg) higher emissions estimated in EDGAR v4.2. The relatively large discrepancy at sector level can be attributed to differences in energy statistics and emission factors (Guan et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2015) as well as differences in sector definitions. In particularly, EDGAR v4.2 used fuel consumption data from IEA statistics while MIX and REAS2 used provincial level data from Chinese Energy Statistics, which can differ by 20% at sector level (Hong et al., 2016). Emissions from heating plants are aggregated to the industrial sector in the MIX inventory, while in EDGAR v4.2 heating plants

are aggregated to the energy sector and then compared to the power sector in the MIX inventory. In the future, harmonizing the sector (subsector) definition among global and regional inventories would help to reduce the discrepancy of emission estimates at sector level.

The agriculture sector is a dominant source of  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions, mainly contributed by fertilizer applications and manure managements. MIX incorporated the PKU-NH $_3$  inventory for China, which estimated agricultural  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions using a process-based model to represent the dynamic impact of fertilizer use patterns, meteorological factors, and soil properties (Huang et al., 2012). The new inventory improved on previous studies which used uniform emission factors across time and region. Table 8 compares agricultural  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions in China estimated in different emission inventories. Compared to other work, PKU-NH $_3$  yields lower estimates for fertilizer application but higher estimates for manure management. The differences are mainly because PKU-NH $_3$  used local correction factors for fertilizer volatilization and manure loss rate (Huang et al., 2012). Top-down inversion of  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions by adjoint model and deposition fluxes agrees well with Huang et al. (2012), confirming the validity of the process-based model (Paulot et al., 2014).

This section further discusses the differences between MIX and REAS2 over China. EDGAR is not compared here because references to the detailed underlying data used in EDGAR are not available. Figure S1 in the Supplement compares MIX and REAS2 estimates for China for 2008 by species and by sector. The two inventories generally agree well given that both MEIC and REAS2 incorporate the most recent advances in emission inventory studies in China. The major differences between the two inventories are discussed below with explanation for possible reasons.

The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).

When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from

For a mosaic emission inventory, inconsistencies could occur at country boarders when emissions of the two adjacent countries were obtained from different datasets (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015). In the MIX inventory, the inconsistencies are expected at the country boarder of China and India. However, low populations and emissions are observed along the border of China, reducing the impact of cross-border grids on the accuracy of emissions. Also, deriving country totals from the gridded emissions is not appropriate for small countries due to the impact from cross-border grids, especially for those grids with large point source emissions (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015).

The current MIX inventory also has several limitations. Firstly, it provides emissions with aggregated sectoral information, which may be sufficient for the base case model but insufficient for targeted policy cases. Secondly, the MIX inventory is provided with moderate spatial resolution (i.e.,  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ ), which could support global and regional models but is still too coarse for urban models. Finally, yet importantly, gridded emissions are only available for 2008 and 2010 to support base years modeling activities in MICS-Asia and HTAP. For other years, modelers could use available global and regional inventories with more complete year coverage (e.g., EDGAR v4.2, REAS2) or extrapolate the gridded MIX inventory to the neighboring years. Developing a complete time series of gridded emission dataset with the

In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. Gridded emissions are available from the following website: http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.

The MIX inventory provides a consistent emission input for Asian regions for global and regional modeling activities. We expect that the MIX inventory can provide a good foundation for air quality modeling and can help to improve the model performance. However, the MIX inventory still has some limitations. It is very difficult to conduct quantitative uncertainty analysis for a mosaic inventory, which limits understanding of the reliability of the MIX inventory. Validation of the MIX inventory could be provided by comparing model predictions with in situ and satellite observations.
